metadata.note: Card 11. Exclusions: no geometric-series average-energy derivation, no mode-counting integral, no historical Planck-vs-Einstein quantization subtlety beyond one line.

beats.narration_text: A glowing oven should contain infinite energy — if classical physics were right. It doesn't. And fixing that prediction broke physics open.

props.headline: A glowing oven should contain infinite energy — if classical physics…

card.headline: Why a Hot Box Doesn't Hold Infinite Energy

beats.narration_text: A hollow box at a fixed temperature holds electromagnetic waves — many modes at many frequencies. Classical thermodynamics assigns every mode the same average energy, kT, by the equipartition theorem. The energy density at each frequency rises as the square of the frequency. Keep summing and you get infinity.

props.headline: A hollow box at a fixed temperature holds electromagnetic waves —…

beats.narration_text: Experiments showed a finite spectrum with a peak: energy rises at low frequencies, hits a maximum, then drops off sharply at high frequencies. There is no catastrophe in nature. Classical physics predicts infinite energy. The real oven doesn't deliver it. Why?

props.headline: Experiments showed a finite spectrum with a peak: energy rises at…

card.headline: Classical physics predicts infinite energy. A real oven has a finite peak. Why?

beats.narration_text: In 1900, Max Planck found a formula that fit the measurements perfectly. His key assumption: the cavity walls can only exchange energy with the field in discrete chunks — multiples of hν, where h is a new constant of nature and ν is frequency. The energy is quantized.

props.headline: In 1900, Max Planck found a formula that fit the measurements…

card.headline: Energy comes in chunks hν — high-frequency modes are starved.

beats.narration_text: At low frequency, hν is tiny compared to thermal energy kT — the classical result is recovered. At high frequency, hν is much larger than kT — the mode cannot afford even one packet. It stays empty. The curve bends back down and the catastrophe disappears.

props.headline: At low frequency, hν is tiny compared to thermal energy kT…

beats.narration_text: The Rayleigh–Jeans curve rises forever — infinite energy at short wavelengths. The Planck curve peaks and falls. The two agree at long wavelengths, where packets are cheap, and diverge at short wavelengths, where the classical picture has no floor.

props.headline: The Rayleigh–Jeans curve rises forever — infinite energy at short wavelengths.…

beats.narration_text: Classical is uncapped. Quantum is not.

props.headline: Classical is uncapped. Quantum is not.

card.headline: Equipartition climbs forever. Quantization bends it back down.
Two frequencies, one temperature, two regimes

beats.narration_text: Illustrative numbers: room temperature T = 300 K gives kT ≈ 0.025 eV. A low-frequency microwave mode at ν = 10 GHz has hν ≈ 0.00004 eV — tiny compared to kT, so the mode is well-populated and the classical and Planck curves agree. A high-frequency UV mode at ν = 10¹⁵ Hz has hν ≈ 4 eV — far larger than kT, so the Boltzmann factor kills the population. No packets fit. The classical curve would assign this mode full kT; Planck assigns it nearly zero. That suppression at high frequency removes the catastrophe.

props.headline: Illustrative numbers: room temperature T = 300 K gives kT ≈…

beats.narration_text: Why doesn't a hot box hold infinite energy? Because energy exchange is quantized. High-frequency modes demand packets too expensive for thermal energy to fill. The Planck distribution is what happens when nature cannot afford the high rungs of the energy ladder. QUANTUM MECHANICS.

props.headline: Why doesn't a hot box hold infinite energy? Because energy exchange…

card.headline: Quantized energy exchange starves high-frequency modes — that is why the catastrophe does not happen.

beats.name: OutroSeries
beats.narration_text: Part of the Quantum Mechanics A Companion Guide series.
